questions.html: When induced emf in inductor coil is 50% of its maximum value then stored energy in inductor coil in the given circuit will be
questions.questionHtml: When induced emf in inductor coil is 50% of its maximum value then stored energy in inductor coil in the given circuit will be
questions.solutionText: Solution At steady state, inductor shorts the 2 Ω branch, so I m a x = 2 1 = 2 A Induced emf = L d I d t , which is 50% of maximum when I = 50 % of I m a x = 1 A U = 1 2 L I 2 = 1 2 × 5 × 10 − 3 × 1 = 2.5 mJ Answer: (1)
questions.solution: Solution At steady state, inductor shorts the 2 Ω branch, so I m a x = 2 1 = 2 A Induced emf = L d I d t , which is 50% of maximum when I = 50 % of I m a x = 1 A U = 1 2 L I 2 = 1 2 × 5 × 10 − 3 × 1 = 2.5 mJ Answer: (1)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
At steady state, inductor shorts the 2Ω branch, so Imax=21=2 A

Induced emf =LdIdt, which is 50% of maximum when I=50% of Imax=1 A

U=12LI2=12×5×10−3×1=2.5 mJ

Answer: (1)

questions.html: There are two forces each having same magnitude 10 N. One is inclined at an angle of 30o and other is inclined at an angle of 135o to the positive direction of x -axis. The x and y components of the resultant are
questions.questionHtml: There are two forces each having same magnitude 10 N. One is inclined at an angle of 30o and other is inclined at an angle of 135o to the positive direction of x -axis. The x and y components of the resultant are
questions.solutionText: Solution After resolving forces in x y - direction, we get Along x direction, net force; F x = 10 cos 30 o + − cos 45 o = 10 3 2 − 1 2 = 1.59 N Along y direction, net force; F y = 10 sin 30 o + 10 sin 45 o F y = 10 ( 1 2 + 1 2 ) = 12.07 N Thus forces in x and y direction are 1.59 N and 12.07 N respectively.
questions.solution: Solution After resolving forces in x y - direction, we get Along x direction, net force; F x = 10 cos 30 o + − cos 45 o = 10 3 2 − 1 2 = 1.59 N Along y direction, net force; F y = 10 sin 30 o + 10 sin 45 o F y = 10 ( 1 2 + 1 2 ) = 12.07 N Thus forces in x and y direction are 1.59 N and 12.07 N respectively.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

After resolving forces in xy- direction, we get
Along x direction, net force;
Fx=10cos30o+−cos45o=1032−12=1.59 N
Along y direction, net force;
Fy=10sin30o+10 sin45o
Fy=10(12+12)
=12.07 N
Thus forces in x and y direction are 1.59 N and 12.07 N respectively.

questions.solutionText: Solution Vertical displacement of the first stone is: = 1 2 g t 2 = 1 2 10 3 2 = 45 m . Similarly, the vertical displacement of the second stone is = 45 m . If the height of stones is h 1 and h 2 after t = 3 s then, h 1 = 1 0 0 - 1 2 g t 2 = 100 - 45 = 5 5 m h 2 = 1 0 0 - 1 2 g t 2 = 5 5 m . Hence the height of the centre of mass is: h C M = m 1 h 1 + m 2 h 2 m 1 + m 2 = 5 5 m .

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In a radioactive material, the activity at time t 1 is R 1 and at a later time t 2 , it is R 2 . If the decay constant of the material is λ , then
questions.questionText: In a radioactive material, the activity at time t 1 is R 1 and at a later time t 2 , it is R 2 . If the decay constant of the material is λ , then
questions.question: In a radioactive material, the activity at time t 1 is R 1 and at a later time t 2 , it is R 2 . If the decay constant of the material is λ , then
questions.prompt: In a radioactive material, the activity at time t 1 is R 1 and at a later time t 2 , it is R 2 . If the decay constant of the material is λ , then
questions.html: In a radioactive material, the activity at time t1 is R1 and at a later time t2, it is R2. If the decay constant of the material is λ, then
questions.questionHtml: In a radioactive material, the activity at time t1 is R1 and at a later time t2, it is R2. If the decay constant of the material is λ, then
questions.solutionText: Solution Activity at any time t is given by R = R 0 e − λ t So: R 1 = R 0 e − λ t 1 and R 2 = R 0 e − λ t 2 Dividing: R 1 R 2 = e − λ ( t 1 − t 2 ) = e λ ( t 2 − t 1 ) ⇒ R 1 = R 2 e λ ( t 2 − t 1 ) = R 2 e − λ ( t 1 − t 2 ) Answer: (2)
questions.solution: Solution Activity at any time t is given by R = R 0 e − λ t So: R 1 = R 0 e − λ t 1 and R 2 = R 0 e − λ t 2 Dividing: R 1 R 2 = e − λ ( t 1 − t 2 ) = e λ ( t 2 − t 1 ) ⇒ R 1 = R 2 e λ ( t 2 − t 1 ) = R 2 e − λ ( t 1 − t 2 ) Answer: (2)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Activity at any time t is given by R=R0e−λt

So: R1=R0e−λt1 and R2=R0e−λt2

Dividing: R1R2=e−λ(t1−t2)=eλ(t2−t1)

⇒R1=R2eλ(t2−t1)=R2e−λ(t1−t2)

Answer: (2)

questions.html: Which of the following is NOT a 'donor' impurity?
questions.questionHtml: Which of the following is NOT a 'donor' impurity?
questions.solutionText: Solution Donor impurities are those which have five electrons in their valence shell. Among the given options Bismuth, Antimony and Arsenic are pentavalent atoms while Iridium has only three electrons in its valence shell. Therefore, it cannot act as a donor impurity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Your friend is having eye sight problem. She is not able to see clearly a distant uniform window mesh and it appears to her as nonuniform and distorted. The doctor diagnosed the problem as :
questions.questionText: Your friend is having eye sight problem. She is not able to see clearly a distant uniform window mesh and it appears to her as nonuniform and distorted. The doctor diagnosed the problem as :
questions.question: Your friend is having eye sight problem. She is not able to see clearly a distant uniform window mesh and it appears to her as nonuniform and distorted. The doctor diagnosed the problem as :
questions.prompt: Your friend is having eye sight problem. She is not able to see clearly a distant uniform window mesh and it appears to her as nonuniform and distorted. The doctor diagnosed the problem as :
questions.html: Your friend is having eye sight problem. She is not able to see clearly a distant uniform window mesh and it appears to her as nonuniform and distorted. The doctor diagnosed the problem as :
questions.questionHtml: Your friend is having eye sight problem. She is not able to see clearly a distant uniform window mesh and it appears to her as nonuniform and distorted. The doctor diagnosed the problem as :
questions.solutionText: Solution The person cannot see a distant object clearly → this indicates Myopia (short-sightedness). The uniform window mesh appears nonuniform and distorted → this indicates Astigmatism (unequal curvature of the cornea/lens causing different focal points in different planes). Since both conditions are present simultaneously, the diagnosis is Myopia with Astigmatism. Answer: (2)
questions.solution: Solution The person cannot see a distant object clearly → this indicates Myopia (short-sightedness). The uniform window mesh appears nonuniform and distorted → this indicates Astigmatism (unequal curvature of the cornea/lens causing different focal points in different planes). Since both conditions are present simultaneously, the diagnosis is Myopia with Astigmatism. Answer: (2)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The person cannot see a distant object clearly → this indicates Myopia (short-sightedness).

The uniform window mesh appears nonuniform and distorted → this indicates Astigmatism (unequal curvature of the cornea/lens causing different focal points in different planes).

Since both conditions are present simultaneously, the diagnosis is Myopia with Astigmatism.

Answer: (2)

questions.questionHtml:
Find the energy equivalent of one atomic mass unit in Joules and in MeV.
(Take c=3×108and 1 amu=1.66×10−24
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
E=mc2=1.66×10−27×(3×108)2

E=1.66×10−27×9×1016=14.94×10−11≈1.5×10−10 J

Converting to MeV: 1.5×10−101.6×10−13=931.5 MeV

Answer: (3)

questions.solutionText: Solution Let the velocity of a particle be v where mass m is double the rest mass i.e., m = 2 m 0 then m 0 = m 1 − v 2 c 2 ⇒ m 0 = 2 m 0 1 − v 2 c 2 1 2 = 1 − v 2 c 2 ⇒ 1 4 = 1 − v 2 c 2 ⇒ v 2 c 2 = 1 − 1 4 = 3 4 ⇒ v = 3 4 c
questions.solution: Solution Let the velocity of a particle be v where mass m is double the rest mass i.e., m = 2 m 0 then m 0 = m 1 − v 2 c 2 ⇒ m 0 = 2 m 0 1 − v 2 c 2 1 2 = 1 − v 2 c 2 ⇒ 1 4 = 1 − v 2 c 2 ⇒ v 2 c 2 = 1 − 1 4 = 3 4 ⇒ v = 3 4 c
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Let the velocity of a particle be v where mass m is double the rest mass i.e., m=2 m0 then
m0=m1−v2c2⇒m0=2 m01−v2c2
12=1−v2c2⇒14=1−v2c2
⇒v2c2=1−14=34
⇒v=34c

questions.solutionText: Solution Col I Match Col II Reason (a) Nuclear fusion p, q Converts matter to energy; occurs in light (low atomic number) nuclei (b) Nuclear fission p, r Converts matter to energy; occurs in heavy (high atomic number) nuclei (c) β -decay p, s Converts matter to energy; mediated by weak nuclear force (d) Exothermic nuclear reaction p, q, r All exothermic nuclear reactions convert matter to energy; possible for both low and high atomic number nuclei Answer: (3)
questions.solution: Solution Col I Match Col II Reason (a) Nuclear fusion p, q Converts matter to energy; occurs in light (low atomic number) nuclei (b) Nuclear fission p, r Converts matter to energy; occurs in heavy (high atomic number) nuclei (c) β -decay p, s Converts matter to energy; mediated by weak nuclear force (d) Exothermic nuclear reaction p, q, r All exothermic nuclear reactions convert matter to energy; possible for both low and high atomic number nuclei Answer: (3)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Col IMatchCol IIReason
(a)Nuclear fusionp, qConverts matter to energy; occurs in light (low atomic number) nuclei
(b)Nuclear fissionp, rConverts matter to energy; occurs in heavy (high atomic number) nuclei
(c)β-decayp, sConverts matter to energy; mediated by weak nuclear force
(d)Exothermic nuclear reactionp, q, rAll exothermic nuclear reactions convert matter to energy; possible for both low and high atomic number nuclei

Answer: (3)

questions.solutionText: Solution When a charged particle of charge q is accelerated through a potential V , then de-Broglie wavelength is given as λ = h 2 m q V ⇒ λ ∝ 1 m q ⇒ λ α λ p = m p m α ⋅ q p q α ⇒ λ α λ = m p 4 m p q p 2 q p [ ∵ m α = 4 m p and q α = 2 q p ] ⇒ λ α λ = 1 8 ⇒ λ α = λ 2 2
questions.solution: Solution When a charged particle of charge q is accelerated through a potential V , then de-Broglie wavelength is given as λ = h 2 m q V ⇒ λ ∝ 1 m q ⇒ λ α λ p = m p m α ⋅ q p q α ⇒ λ α λ = m p 4 m p q p 2 q p [ ∵ m α = 4 m p and q α = 2 q p ] ⇒ λ α λ = 1 8 ⇒ λ α = λ 2 2
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
When a charged particle of charge q is accelerated through a potential V, then de-Broglie wavelength is given as
λ=h2mqV
⇒λ∝1mq
⇒λαλp=mpmα⋅qpqα
⇒λαλ=mp4mpqp2qp[∵mα=4mp and qα=2qp]
⇒λαλ=18
⇒λα=λ22

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An electron with kinetic energy 10 eV is incident on a hydrogen atom in its ground state. The collision
questions.questionText: An electron with kinetic energy 10 eV is incident on a hydrogen atom in its ground state. The collision
questions.question: An electron with kinetic energy 10 eV is incident on a hydrogen atom in its ground state. The collision
questions.prompt: An electron with kinetic energy 10 eV is incident on a hydrogen atom in its ground state. The collision
questions.html: An electron with kinetic energy 10 eV is incident on a hydrogen atom in its ground state. The collision
questions.questionHtml: An electron with kinetic energy 10 eV is incident on a hydrogen atom in its ground state. The collision
questions.solutionText: Solution Ground state energy of hydrogen atom = − 13.6 eV Energy required for first excitation (n=1 → n=2): 13.6 ( 1 − 1 4 ) = 10.2 eV The incident electron has kinetic energy = 10 eV, which is less than 10.2 eV. Since the electron does not have sufficient energy to excite the hydrogen atom to any higher energy level, no energy can be absorbed by the atom. Therefore the collision must be elastic. Answer: (1) ^
questions.solution: Solution Ground state energy of hydrogen atom = − 13.6 eV Energy required for first excitation (n=1 → n=2): 13.6 ( 1 − 1 4 ) = 10.2 eV The incident electron has kinetic energy = 10 eV, which is less than 10.2 eV. Since the electron does not have sufficient energy to excite the hydrogen atom to any higher energy level, no energy can be absorbed by the atom. Therefore the collision must be elastic. Answer: (1) ^
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Ground state energy of hydrogen atom =−13.6 eV

Energy required for first excitation (n=1 → n=2): 13.6(1−14)=10.2 eV

The incident electron has kinetic energy =10 eV, which is less than 10.2 eV.

Since the electron does not have sufficient energy to excite the hydrogen atom to any higher energy level, no energy can be absorbed by the atom.

Therefore the collision must be elastic.

Answer: (1)
^

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In a Rutherford scattering experiment, an a-particle of initial kinetic energy 1 0 M e V is directed normally toward a stationary nucleus of charge 100 e − . If the closest distance of approach equals the nuclear radius, the size of the nucleus is:
questions.questionText: In a Rutherford scattering experiment, an a-particle of initial kinetic energy 1 0 M e V is directed normally toward a stationary nucleus of charge 100 e − . If the closest distance of approach equals the nuclear radius, the size of the nucleus is:
questions.question: In a Rutherford scattering experiment, an a-particle of initial kinetic energy 1 0 M e V is directed normally toward a stationary nucleus of charge 100 e − . If the closest distance of approach equals the nuclear radius, the size of the nucleus is:
questions.prompt: In a Rutherford scattering experiment, an a-particle of initial kinetic energy 1 0 M e V is directed normally toward a stationary nucleus of charge 100 e − . If the closest distance of approach equals the nuclear radius, the size of the nucleus is:
questions.html: In a Rutherford scattering experiment, an a-particle of initial kinetic energy 10 MeV is directed normally toward a stationary nucleus of charge 100e−. If the closest distance of approach equals the nuclear radius, the size of the nucleus is:
questions.questionHtml: In a Rutherford scattering experiment, an a-particle of initial kinetic energy 10 MeV is directed normally toward a stationary nucleus of charge 100e−. If the closest distance of approach equals the nuclear radius, the size of the nucleus is:
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
At closest approach, all KE converts to PE:

KE=14πε0⋅q1q2r

q1=2e (alpha particle), q2=100e

KE=10 MeV=10×1.6×10−13 J=1.6×10−12 J

r=9×109×2×1.6×10−19×100×1.6×10−191.6×10−12

r=9×109×2×100×(1.6)2×10−381.6×10−12

r=9×109×200×2.56×10−381.6×10−12

r=9×200×2.561.6×109−38+12

r=46081.6×10−17=2880×10−17≈3×10−14 m

Answer: (2) 3×10−14 m
`

questions.text: 22 g of C O 2 at 27 ° C is mixed with 16 g of O 2 at 37 ° C . If both gases are considered as ideal then the temperature of the mixture is (ignore vibrational degree of freedom)
questions.questionText: 22 g of C O 2 at 27 ° C is mixed with 16 g of O 2 at 37 ° C . If both gases are considered as ideal then the temperature of the mixture is (ignore vibrational degree of freedom)
questions.question: 22 g of C O 2 at 27 ° C is mixed with 16 g of O 2 at 37 ° C . If both gases are considered as ideal then the temperature of the mixture is (ignore vibrational degree of freedom)
questions.prompt: 22 g of C O 2 at 27 ° C is mixed with 16 g of O 2 at 37 ° C . If both gases are considered as ideal then the temperature of the mixture is (ignore vibrational degree of freedom)
questions.html: 22 g of CO2 at 27 °C is mixed with 16 g of O2 at 37 °C. If both gases are considered as ideal then the temperature of the mixture is (ignore vibrational degree of freedom)
questions.questionHtml: 22 g of CO2 at 27 °C is mixed with 16 g of O2 at 37 °C. If both gases are considered as ideal then the temperature of the mixture is (ignore vibrational degree of freedom)
questions.solutionText: Solution Moles: n C O 2 = 22 44 = 0.5 mol, n O 2 = 16 32 = 0.5 mol Degrees of freedom: C O 2 is linear triatomic ⇒ f 1 = 5 , O 2 is diatomic ⇒ f 2 = 5 Using energy conservation: n 1 f 1 T 1 + n 2 f 2 T 2 = ( n 1 f 1 + n 2 f 2 ) T 0.5 × 5 × 300 + 0.5 × 5 × 310 = ( 0.5 × 5 + 0.5 × 5 ) T 750 + 775 = 5 T T = 1525 5 = 305 K = 32 ∘ C Answer: (4) `
questions.solution: Solution Moles: n C O 2 = 22 44 = 0.5 mol, n O 2 = 16 32 = 0.5 mol Degrees of freedom: C O 2 is linear triatomic ⇒ f 1 = 5 , O 2 is diatomic ⇒ f 2 = 5 Using energy conservation: n 1 f 1 T 1 + n 2 f 2 T 2 = ( n 1 f 1 + n 2 f 2 ) T 0.5 × 5 × 300 + 0.5 × 5 × 310 = ( 0.5 × 5 + 0.5 × 5 ) T 750 + 775 = 5 T T = 1525 5 = 305 K = 32 ∘ C Answer: (4) `
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Moles: nCO2=2244=0.5 mol, nO2=1632=0.5 mol

Degrees of freedom: CO2 is linear triatomic ⇒f1=5, O2 is diatomic ⇒f2=5

Using energy conservation:
n1f1T1+n2f2T2=(n1f1+n2f2)T

0.5×5×300+0.5×5×310=(0.5×5+0.5×5)T

750+775=5T

T=15255=305 K =32∘C

Answer: (4)
`

questions.solutionText: Solution Let m be the mass of the sphere. Since, it is a case of backward slipping, force of friction is in forward direction. Limiting friction will act in this case. Linear acceleration a = f m = μ mg m = μ g Angular retardation α = τ I = f · r 2 5 m r 2 = 5 2 μ g r Slipping will be stopped when v = r ω a t = r ( ω 0 - α t ) μ g t = r ( ω 0 - 5 2 μ g t r ) 7 2 μ g t = r ω 0 t = 2 7 r ω 0 μ g ^
questions.solution: Solution Let m be the mass of the sphere. Since, it is a case of backward slipping, force of friction is in forward direction. Limiting friction will act in this case. Linear acceleration a = f m = μ mg m = μ g Angular retardation α = τ I = f · r 2 5 m r 2 = 5 2 μ g r Slipping will be stopped when v = r ω a t = r ( ω 0 - α t ) μ g t = r ( ω 0 - 5 2 μ g t r ) 7 2 μ g t = r ω 0 t = 2 7 r ω 0 μ g ^
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Let m be the mass of the sphere.

Since, it is a case of backward slipping, force of friction is in forward direction. Limiting friction will act in this case.



Linear acceleration a=fm=μmgm=μg

Angular retardation α=τI=f·r25mr2=52μgr

Slipping will be stopped when v=rω
at=r(ω0 - αt)

μgt=r(ω0-52μgtr)

72μgt=rω0

t=27rω0μg
^

questions.text: Compute the bulk modulus of water from the following data: Initial volume = 100 . 0 litre , Pressure increase = 100 . 0 atm ( 1 atm = 1 .013 × 10 5 Pa ) , Final volume = 99 . 5 litre .
questions.questionText: Compute the bulk modulus of water from the following data: Initial volume = 100 . 0 litre , Pressure increase = 100 . 0 atm ( 1 atm = 1 .013 × 10 5 Pa ) , Final volume = 99 . 5 litre .

questions.html: Compute the bulk modulus of water from the following data: Initial volume = 100.0 litre, Pressure increase = 100.0 atm (1 atm=1.013×105 Pa), Final volume = 99.5 litre.
questions.questionHtml: Compute the bulk modulus of water from the following data: Initial volume = 100.0 litre, Pressure increase = 100.0 atm (1 atm=1.013×105 Pa), Final volume = 99.5 litre.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Bulk modulus B=−ΔPΔV/V (magnitude taken since B>0)

ΔP=100 atm=100×1.013×105=1.013×107 Pa

V=100.0 litre, ΔV=100.0−99.5=0.5 litre

ΔVV=0.5100=5×10−3

B=1.013×1075×10−3=1.0135×1010=0.2026×1010

B=2.026×109 Pa

Answer: (1) 2.026×109 Pa
.

questions.solutionText: Solution Output voltage V o = 2 V, R C = 2 K Ω , R B = 1 K Ω , β = 100 Output current (collector current): I C = V o R C = 2 2000 = 1 × 10 − 3 A Base current: I B = I C β = 10 − 3 100 = 10 − 5 A Input signal voltage: V i = I B × R B = 10 − 5 × 1000 = 10 − 2 V = 10 mV Answer: (2) 10 mV *
questions.solution: Solution Output voltage V o = 2 V, R C = 2 K Ω , R B = 1 K Ω , β = 100 Output current (collector current): I C = V o R C = 2 2000 = 1 × 10 − 3 A Base current: I B = I C β = 10 − 3 100 = 10 − 5 A Input signal voltage: V i = I B × R B = 10 − 5 × 1000 = 10 − 2 V = 10 mV Answer: (2) 10 mV *
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Output voltage Vo=2 V, RC=2 KΩ, RB=1 KΩ, β=100

Output current (collector current):
IC=VoRC=22000=1×10−3 A

Base current:
IB=ICβ=10−3100=10−5 A

Input signal voltage:
Vi=IB×RB=10−5×1000=10−2 V =10 mV

Answer: (2) 10 mV
*

questions.solutionText: Solution In general, the current is, i = n e A v d Current density is, J = i A = n e v d For electrons, the current density is, j e = n e V d e = ( 5 × 10 7 × 10 6 ) ( 1 . 6 × 10 - 19 ) ( 0 . 4 ) = 3 . 2 × 10 - 6 A m - 2 The total current density for the material is, J t o t a l = J e + J i o n 4 × 10 - 6 = 3 . 2 × 10 - 6 + J ion J i o n = 0 . 8 × 10 - 6 A m 2 Drift velocity for ions is, ( V d ) i o n = J i o n n e = 0 . 8 × 10 - 6 5 × 10 13 × 1 . 6 × 10 - 19 = 0 . 1 m s - 1 !
questions.solution: Solution In general, the current is, i = n e A v d Current density is, J = i A = n e v d For electrons, the current density is, j e = n e V d e = ( 5 × 10 7 × 10 6 ) ( 1 . 6 × 10 - 19 ) ( 0 . 4 ) = 3 . 2 × 10 - 6 A m - 2 The total current density for the material is, J t o t a l = J e + J i o n 4 × 10 - 6 = 3 . 2 × 10 - 6 + J ion J i o n = 0 . 8 × 10 - 6 A m 2 Drift velocity for ions is, ( V d ) i o n = J i o n n e = 0 . 8 × 10 - 6 5 × 10 13 × 1 . 6 × 10 - 19 = 0 . 1 m s - 1 !
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

In general, the current is,
i=neAvd

Current density is,
J=iA=nevd

For electrons, the current density is,
je=neVde

=(5×107×106)(1.6×10-19)(0.4)

=3.2×10-6 A m-2

The total current density for the material is,

Jtotal=Je+Jion

4×10-6=3.2×10-6+Jion

Jion=0.8×10-6 A m2

Drift velocity for ions is,

(Vd)ion=Jionne=0.8×10-65×1013×1.6×10-19
=0.1 m s-1


!

questions.solutionText: Solution The proof of quantization of energy states in an atom is obtained by the experiment performed by Franck and Hertz with the help of accelerated electron passing through mercury vapour. At certain accelerating voltage of electron, sudden drop in current occur due to observation energy of colliding electron with mercury atom to send it to exited state. Since, it happens only at some certain voltage, this gives the proof of quantization of energy states. :

At certain accelerating voltage of electron, sudden drop in current occur due to observation energy of colliding electron with mercury atom to send it to exited state. questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The proof of quantization of energy states in an atom is obtained by the experiment performed by Franck and Hertz with the help of accelerated electron passing through mercury vapour. At certain accelerating voltage of electron, sudden drop in current occur due to observation energy of colliding electron with mercury atom to send it to exited state.
Since, it happens only at some certain voltage, this gives the proof of quantization of energy states.
:

questions.solutionText: Solution Initially, there will be no voltage drop across capacitor, so intensity of bulb will rise sharply and gradually voltage drop across capacitor will increase as a result voltage drop across bulb decreases, so intensity of bulb will then gradually decrease.

questions.text: A voltage regulator circuit is designed using a Zener diode with a breakdown voltage of V Z = 6 V. The input unregulated DC voltage ( V i n ) fluctuates between 10 V and 15 V. The circuit consists of a series resistor R S = 200 Ω and is connected to a load resistor R L = 1 k Ω . What is the maximum power dissipated by the Zener diode in this circuit?
questions.questionText: A voltage regulator circuit is designed using a Zener diode with a breakdown voltage of V Z = 6 V. The input unregulated DC voltage ( V i n ) fluctuates between 10 V and 15 V. questions.question: A voltage regulator circuit is designed using a Zener diode with a breakdown voltage of V Z = 6 V. The input unregulated DC voltage ( V i n ) fluctuates between 10 V and 15 V. The circuit consists of a series resistor R S = 200 Ω and is connected to a load resistor R L = 1 k Ω . What is the maximum power dissipated by the Zener diode in this circuit?
questions.prompt: A voltage regulator circuit is designed using a Zener diode with a breakdown voltage of V Z = 6 V. The input unregulated DC voltage ( V i n ) fluctuates between 10 V and 15 V. The circuit consists of a series resistor R S = 200 Ω and is connected to a load resistor R L = 1 k Ω . What is the maximum power dissipated by the Zener diode in this circuit?
questions.html:
A voltage regulator circuit is designed using a Zener diode with a breakdown voltage of VZ=6 V. The input unregulated DC voltage (Vin) fluctuates between 10 V and 15 V. The circuit consists of a series resistor RS=200 Ω and is connected to a load resistor RL=1 kΩ.

What is the maximum power dissipated by the Zener diode in this circuit?
questions.questionHtml:
A voltage regulator circuit is designed using a Zener diode with a breakdown voltage of VZ=6 V. The input unregulated DC voltage (Vin) fluctuates between 10 V and 15 V. The circuit consists of a series resistor RS=200 Ω and is connected to a load resistor RL=1 kΩ.

What is the maximum power dissipated by the Zener diode in this circuit?
questions.solutionText: Solution Zener maintains V Z = 6 V across load. Load current (constant): I L = V Z R L = 6 1000 = 6 mA Power dissipated by Zener is maximum when V i n is maximum = 15 V. Current through R S : I S = V i n − V Z R S = 15 − 6 200 = 9 200 = 45 mA Zener current: I Z = I S − I L = 45 − 6 = 39 mA Maximum power dissipated by Zener: P Z = V Z × I Z = 6 × 39 × 10 − 3 = 234 mW Answer: B) 234 mW
questions.solution: Solution Zener maintains V Z = 6 V across load. Load current (constant): I L = V Z R L = 6 1000 = 6 mA Power dissipated by Zener is maximum when V i n is maximum = 15 V. Current through R S : I S = V i n − V Z R S = 15 − 6 200 = 9 200 = 45 mA Zener current: I Z = I S − I L = 45 − 6 = 39 mA Maximum power dissipated by Zener: P Z = V Z × I Z = 6 × 39 × 10 − 3 = 234 mW Answer: B) 234 mW
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Zener maintains VZ=6 V across load.

Load current (constant):
IL=VZRL=61000=6 mA

Power dissipated by Zener is maximum when Vin is maximum =15 V.

Current through RS:
IS=Vin−VZRS=15−6200=9200=45 mA

Zener current:
IZ=IS−IL=45−6=39 mA

Maximum power dissipated by Zener:
PZ=VZ×IZ=6×39×10−3=234 mW

Answer: B) 234 mW

questions.questionHtml: A vibration magnetometer consists of two identical bar magnets placed one over the other such that they are perpendicular and bisect each other. The time period of oscillation in a horizontal magnetic field 25/4 s. One of the magnets is removed and if the other magnet oscillates in the same field, then the time period in seconds is :
questions.solutionText: Solution Time period of magnet in vibration magnetometer, T = 2 π I M H First case, T 1 = 2 π I 1 + I 2 M ′ H where, M = resultant magnetic moment of two magnets Here, two identical magnets are placed perpendicular to each other. ∴ I 1 = I 2 = I (Let) and M ′ = M 2 + M 2 = M 2 ∴ T 1 = 2 π 2 I 2 M H 2 5 / 4 = 2 π 2 I 2 M H … (i) When one magnet is removed, then time period, T 2 = 2 π I M H ...(ii) On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get 2 5 / 4 T 2 = 2 2 = 2 2 5 / 4 T 2 = 2 1 / 4 T 2 = 2 5 / 4 2 1 / 4 = 2 s
questions.solution: Solution Time period of magnet in vibration magnetometer, T = 2 π I M H First case, T 1 = 2 π I 1 + I 2 M ′ H where, M = resultant magnetic moment of two magnets Here, two identical magnets are placed perpendicular to each other. ∴ I 1 = I 2 = I (Let) and M ′ = M 2 + M 2 = M 2 ∴ T 1 = 2 π 2 I 2 M H 2 5 / 4 = 2 π 2 I 2 M H … (i) When one magnet is removed, then time period, T 2 = 2 π I M H ...(ii) On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get 2 5 / 4 T 2 = 2 2 = 2 2 5 / 4 T 2 = 2 1 / 4 T 2 = 2 5 / 4 2 1 / 4 = 2 s
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Time period of magnet in vibration magnetometer,
T=2πIMH
First case, T1=2πI1+I2M′H
where, M= resultant magnetic moment of two magnets
Here, two identical magnets are placed perpendicular to each other.
∴I1=I2=I (Let)
and M′=M2+M2=M2
∴T1=2π2I2MH
25/4=2π2I2MH … (i)
When one magnet is removed, then time period,
T2=2πIMH ...(ii)
On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get
25/4T2=22=2
25/4T2=21/4
T2=25/421/4=2 s

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Heat is supplied to a diatomic gas at constant pressure. The ratio of ∆ Q : ∆ U : ∆ W is
questions.questionText: Heat is supplied to a diatomic gas at constant pressure. The ratio of ∆ Q : ∆ U : ∆ W is
questions.question: Heat is supplied to a diatomic gas at constant pressure. The ratio of ∆ Q : ∆ U : ∆ W is
questions.prompt: Heat is supplied to a diatomic gas at constant pressure. The ratio of ∆ Q : ∆ U : ∆ W is
questions.html: Heat is supplied to a diatomic gas at constant pressure. The ratio of ∆Q :∆U :∆W is
questions.questionHtml: Heat is supplied to a diatomic gas at constant pressure. The ratio of ∆Q :∆U :∆W is
questions.solutionText: Solution The molar specific heat at constant pressure is C P = ∆ Q n ∆ T ∆ Q = C P n ∆ T At constant pressure and for diatomicgas, C v = 5 R 2 C P = C v + R ⇒ C v = 7 R 2 and γ = C P C v ⇒ γ = 7 5 So, now ∆ Q = 7 R 2 × n ∆ T ⇒ ∆ Q = 7 2 n R ∆ T … ( 1 ) Also, we know work done, ∆ W = P ∆ V ⇒ ∆ W = n R ∆ T … ( 2 ) Also, ∆ U = ∆ Q - ∆ W = 7 2 - 1 n R ∆ T ⇒ ∆ U = 5 2 n R ∆ T … ( 3 ) ∆ Q : ∆ U : ∆ W = ( 1 ) : ( 3 ) : ( 2 ) = 7 2 n R ∆ T : 5 2 n R ∆ T : n R ∆ T = 7 : 5 : 2
questions.solution: Solution The molar specific heat at constant pressure is C P = ∆ Q n ∆ T ∆ Q = C P n ∆ T At constant pressure and for diatomicgas, C v = 5 R 2 C P = C v + R ⇒ C v = 7 R 2 and γ = C P C v ⇒ γ = 7 5 So, now ∆ Q = 7 R 2 × n ∆ T ⇒ ∆ Q = 7 2 n R ∆ T … ( 1 ) Also, we know work done, ∆ W = P ∆ V ⇒ ∆ W = n R ∆ T … ( 2 ) Also, ∆ U = ∆ Q - ∆ W = 7 2 - 1 n R ∆ T ⇒ ∆ U = 5 2 n R ∆ T … ( 3 ) ∆ Q : ∆ U : ∆ W = ( 1 ) : ( 3 ) : ( 2 ) = 7 2 n R ∆ T : 5 2 n R ∆ T : n R ∆ T = 7 : 5 : 2
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

The molar specific heat at constant pressure is

CP=∆Qn∆T∆Q=CPn∆T

At constant pressure and for diatomicgas, Cv=5R2CP=Cv+ R⇒Cv=7R2and γ=CPCv⇒γ=75

So, now

∆Q = 7R2 × n ∆T ⇒∆Q= 72nR∆T …(1)

Also, we know work done,

∆W=P∆V⇒∆W=nR∆T …(2)

Also,

∆U=∆Q-∆W=72-1nR∆T⇒∆U=52nR∆T …(3)
∆Q :∆U :∆W=(1):(3):(2)=72nR∆T:52nR∆T:nR∆T=7:5:2

questions.html:
Give the correct order of initials T or F for following statements. Use T if statement is true and F if it is false
(i) In Gold Schmidt thermite process aluminium acts as a reducing agent.
(ii) Mg is extracted by electrolysis of aqueous solution of MgCl2
(iii) Extraction of Pb is possible by carbon reduction method
(iv) Red Bauxite is purified by Serpeck’s process
questions.questionHtml:
Give the correct order of initials T or F for following statements. Use T if statement is true and F if it is false
(i) In Gold Schmidt thermite process aluminium acts as a reducing agent.
(ii) Mg is extracted by electrolysis of aqueous solution of MgCl2
(iii) Extraction of Pb is possible by carbon reduction method
(iv) Red Bauxite is purified by Serpeck’s process
questions.solutionText: Solution (i) In Gold Schmidt thermite process, aluminium reduces the metal oxide, so Al acts as a reducing agent. → T (ii) Mg cannot be extracted by electrolysis of aqueous M g C l 2 because water gets discharged preferentially at the cathode. Mg is extracted by electrolysis of fused M g C l 2 . → F (iii) Pb has low reactivity and can be extracted by carbon reduction of P b S (after roasting to P b O ). → T (iv) Red Bauxite is purified by Baeyer's process, not Serpeck's process. Serpeck's process is used for white bauxite containing silica impurity. → F Answer: (4) TFTF
questions.solution: Solution (i) In Gold Schmidt thermite process, aluminium reduces the metal oxide, so Al acts as a reducing agent. → T (ii) Mg cannot be extracted by electrolysis of aqueous M g C l 2 because water gets discharged preferentially at the cathode. Mg is extracted by electrolysis of fused M g C l 2 . → F (iii) Pb has low reactivity and can be extracted by carbon reduction of P b S (after roasting to P b O ). → T (iv) Red Bauxite is purified by Baeyer's process, not Serpeck's process. Serpeck's process is used for white bauxite containing silica impurity. → F Answer: (4) TFTF
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
(i) In Gold Schmidt thermite process, aluminium reduces the metal oxide, so Al acts as a reducing agent. → T

(ii) Mg cannot be extracted by electrolysis of aqueous MgCl2 because water gets discharged preferentially at the cathode. Mg is extracted by electrolysis of fused MgCl2. → F

(iii) Pb has low reactivity and can be extracted by carbon reduction of PbS (after roasting to PbO). → T

(iv) Red Bauxite is purified by Baeyer's process, not Serpeck's process. Serpeck's process is used for white bauxite containing silica impurity. → F

Answer: (4) TFTF

questions.text: Diborane reacts with ammonia to form X , which on heating gives H 2 and borazine. X is
questions.questionText: Diborane reacts with ammonia to form X , which on heating gives H 2 and borazine. X is
questions.question: Diborane reacts with ammonia to form X , which on heating gives H 2 and borazine. X is
questions.prompt: Diborane reacts with ammonia to form X , which on heating gives H 2 and borazine. X is
questions.html: Diborane reacts with ammonia to form X, which on heating gives H2 and borazine. X is
questions.questionHtml: Diborane reacts with ammonia to form X, which on heating gives H2 and borazine. X is
questions.solutionText: Solution B 2 H 6 + 2 N H 3 → [ B H 2 ( N H 3 ) 2 ] + [ B H 4 ] − (at low temperature) This is the ionic product X , formed by unsymmetrical cleavage of diborane. On heating X : [ B H 2 ( N H 3 ) 2 ] + [ B H 4 ] − → B 3 N 3 H 6 (borazine) + H 2 This confirms X = [ B H 2 ( N H 3 ) 2 ] + [ B H 4 ] − Answer: (1)
questions.solution: Solution B 2 H 6 + 2 N H 3 → [ B H 2 ( N H 3 ) 2 ] + [ B H 4 ] − (at low temperature) This is the ionic product X , formed by unsymmetrical cleavage of diborane. On heating X : [ B H 2 ( N H 3 ) 2 ] + [ B H 4 ] − → B 3 N 3 H 6 (borazine) + H 2 This confirms X = [ B H 2 ( N H 3 ) 2 ] + [ B H 4 ] − Answer: (1)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
B2H6+2NH3→[BH2(NH3)2]+[BH4]− (at low temperature)

This is the ionic product X, formed by unsymmetrical cleavage of diborane.

On heating X:
[BH2(NH3)2]+[BH4]−→B3N3H6 (borazine) +H2

This confirms X=[BH2(NH3)2]+[BH4]−

Answer: (1)

questions.text: Which of the following ores are best concentrated by Froth Flotation method?
questions.questionText: Which of the following ores are best concentrated by Froth Flotation method?
questions.question: Which of the following ores are best concentrated by Froth Flotation method?
questions.prompt: Which of the following ores are best concentrated by Froth Flotation method?
questions.html: Which of the following ores are best concentrated by Froth Flotation method?
questions.questionHtml: Which of the following ores are best concentrated by Froth Flotation method?
questions.solutionText: Solution Froth Flotation: This process is especially suitable for the concentration of sulphide ores. The process is based on the difference in wetting characteristics of the ore and gangue particles with water and oil. Magnetite FeO · Fe 2 O 3 ( oxide ore ) Cassiterite SnO 2 ( oxide ore ) Cinnabar HgS ( sulphide ore ) Malachite Cu 2 CO 3 ( OH ) 2 ( oxide carbonate ore ) Sulphide ores are concentrated by Froth Flotation method. Therefore, cinnabar can be concentrated by this method.
questions.solution: Solution Froth Flotation: This process is especially suitable for the concentration of sulphide ores. The process is based on the difference in wetting characteristics of the ore and gangue particles with water and oil. Magnetite FeO · Fe 2 O 3 ( oxide ore ) Cassiterite SnO 2 ( oxide ore ) Cinnabar HgS ( sulphide ore ) Malachite Cu 2 CO 3 ( OH ) 2 ( oxide carbonate ore ) Sulphide ores are concentrated by Froth Flotation method. Therefore, cinnabar can be concentrated by this method.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Froth Flotation: This process is especially suitable for the concentration of sulphide ores. The process is based on the difference in wetting characteristics of the ore and gangue particles with water and oil.

Magnetite FeO·Fe2O3 (oxide ore)

Cassiterite SnO2 (oxide ore)

Cinnabar HgS (sulphide ore)

Malachite Cu2CO3(OH)2 (oxide carbonate ore)

Sulphide ores are concentrated by Froth Flotation method. Therefore, cinnabar can be concentrated by this method.

Hence, the structure of X e F 2 becomes linear in accordance with VSEPR theory. B. 2 X e F 2 + 2 H 2 O → 2 X e + 4 H F + O 2 C. ( i ) X e F 2 + 2 H C l → 2 H F + X e + C l 2 ( i i ) X e F 2 + 2 K I → X e + 2 K F + I 2
questions.solution: Solution It acts as a F - donor and forms complexes with covalent fluorides like P F 5 , A s F 5 and S b F 5 . X e F 2 + P F 5 → [ X e F ] + [ P F 6 ] - A. the five electrons pairs form trigonal bipyramid with three lone pairs in equatorial positions. Hence, the structure of X e F 2 becomes linear in accordance with VSEPR theory. B. 2 X e F 2 + 2 H 2 O → 2 X e + 4 H F + O 2 C. Solution
It acts as a F- donor and forms complexes with covalent fluorides like PF5,AsF5 and SbF5 .
XeF2+PF5→[XeF]+[PF6]-

B. 2XeF2+2H2O→2Xe+4HF+O2

(ii) XeF2+2KI→Xe+2KF+I2

questions.text: The primary product formed by the monobromination of phenyl benzoate is

questions.question: The primary product formed by the monobromination of phenyl benzoate is

questions.html: The primary product formed by the monobromination of phenyl benzoate is
questions.questionHtml: The primary product formed by the monobromination of phenyl benzoate is
questions.solutionText: Solution In phenyl benzoate, the 1 st ring gets deactivated, while 2 nd ring gets activated due to lone pair of electrons on the oxygen atom. So it can give ortho -or para- bromo product. But the ortho product is less likely formed as oxygen is -I group, hence, it will provide para- compound as the primary product.
questions.solution: Solution In phenyl benzoate, the 1 st ring gets deactivated, while 2 nd ring gets activated due to lone pair of electrons on the oxygen atom. So it can give ortho -or para- bromo product. But the ortho product is less likely formed as oxygen is -I group, hence, it will provide para- compound as the primary product.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

In phenyl benzoate, the 1string gets deactivated, while 2nd ring gets activated due to lone pair of electrons on the oxygen atom. So it can give ortho -or para- bromo product. But the ortho product is less likely formed as oxygen is -I group, hence, it will provide para- compound as the primary product.

questions.text: Identify the correct decreasing order of ionic radii of trivalent ions ( L n 3 + ) ions from the following:
questions.questionText: Identify the correct decreasing order of ionic radii of trivalent ions ( L n 3 + ) ions from the following:
questions.question: Identify the correct decreasing order of ionic radii of trivalent ions ( L n 3 + ) ions from the following:
questions.prompt: Identify the correct decreasing order of ionic radii of trivalent ions ( L n 3 + ) ions from the following:
questions.html: Identify the correct decreasing order of ionic radii of trivalent ions (Ln3+) ions from the following:
questions.questionHtml: Identify the correct decreasing order of ionic radii of trivalent ions (Ln3+) ions from the following:
questions.solutionText: Solution Lanthanide contraction: as atomic number increases across lanthanides, ionic radius decreases due to poor shielding by 4 f electrons. Atomic numbers: Ce (58) < Pm (61) < Tm (69) < Yb (70) So ionic radii order (decreasing): C e 3 + > P m 3 + > T m 3 + > Y b 3 + Answer: (2) Ce > Pm > Tm > Yb
questions.solution: Solution Lanthanide contraction: as atomic number increases across lanthanides, ionic radius decreases due to poor shielding by 4 f electrons. Atomic numbers: Ce (58) < Pm (61) < Tm (69) < Yb (70) So ionic radii order (decreasing): C e 3 + > P m 3 + > T m 3 + > Y b 3 + Answer: (2) Ce > Pm > Tm > Yb
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Lanthanide contraction: as atomic number increases across lanthanides, ionic radius decreases due to poor shielding by 4f electrons.

Atomic numbers: Ce (58) < Pm (61) < Tm (69) < Yb (70)

So ionic radii order (decreasing):
Ce3+>Pm3+>Tm3+>Yb3+

Answer: (2) Ce > Pm > Tm > Yb

questions.text: Which of the following species contains at least one atom that cannot be described as satisfying the Lewis Octet Rule, even when considering coordinate (dative) bonding or formal charge minimization?
questions.questionText: Which of the following species contains at least one atom that cannot be described as satisfying the Lewis Octet Rule, even when considering coordinate (dative) bonding or formal charge minimization?
questions.question: Which of the following species contains at least one atom that cannot be described as satisfying the Lewis Octet Rule, even when considering coordinate (dative) bonding or formal charge minimization?
questions.prompt: Which of the following species contains at least one atom that cannot be described as satisfying the Lewis Octet Rule, even when considering coordinate (dative) bonding or formal charge minimization?
questions.html: Which of the following species contains at least one atom that cannot be described as satisfying the Lewis Octet Rule, even when considering coordinate (dative) bonding or formal charge minimization?
questions.questionHtml: Which of the following species contains at least one atom that cannot be described as satisfying the Lewis Octet Rule, even when considering coordinate (dative) bonding or formal charge minimization?
questions.solutionText: Solution A) C O : Carbon has a triple bond with coordinate bond from O → C, giving both atoms an octet. Octet satisfied. B) N O 2 + : N forms two double bonds with the two O atoms. N has octet, both O have octet. Octet satisfied. D) O 3 : Central O has one double bond, one single bond, and one lone pair — octet satisfied via resonance. C) I 3 − : Central iodine has 3 lone pairs + 2 bond pairs = 10 electrons around it. This is an expanded octet (hypervalent), which cannot be described by the Lewis octet rule even with dative bonding or formal charge minimization. Iodine exceeds octet with 10 electrons. Answer: C) I 3 −
questions.solution: Solution A) C O : Carbon has a triple bond with coordinate bond from O → C, giving both atoms an octet. Octet satisfied. B) N O 2 + : N forms two double bonds with the two O atoms. N has octet, both O have octet. Octet satisfied. D) O 3 : Central O has one double bond, one single bond, and one lone pair — octet satisfied via resonance. C) I 3 − : Central iodine has 3 lone pairs + 2 bond pairs = 10 electrons around it. This is an expanded octet (hypervalent), which cannot be described by the Lewis octet rule even with dative bonding or formal charge minimization. Iodine exceeds octet with 10 electrons. Answer: C) I 3 −
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
A) CO: Carbon has a triple bond with coordinate bond from O → C, giving both atoms an octet. Octet satisfied.

B) NO2+: N forms two double bonds with the two O atoms. N has octet, both O have octet. Octet satisfied.

D) O3: Central O has one double bond, one single bond, and one lone pair — octet satisfied via resonance.

C) I3−: Central iodine has 3 lone pairs + 2 bond pairs = 10 electrons around it. This is an expanded octet (hypervalent), which cannot be described by the Lewis octet rule even with dative bonding or formal charge minimization. Iodine exceeds octet with 10 electrons.

Answer: C) I3−

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements are true? I. Ferrimagnetic material, Fe 3 O 4 becomes paramagnetic at 850 K . II. Paramagnetic behaviour of a material is due to the presence of the unpaired electrons in it. III. The maximum coordination number in ionic solids is 8 .
questions.question: Which of the following statements are true? I. Ferrimagnetic material, Fe 3 O 4 becomes paramagnetic at 850 K . II. Paramagnetic behaviour of a material is due to the presence of the unpaired electrons in it. III. The maximum coordination number in ionic solids is 8 .
questions.prompt: Which of the following statements are true? I. Ferrimagnetic material, Fe 3 O 4 becomes paramagnetic at 850 K . II. Paramagnetic behaviour of a material is due to the presence of the unpaired electrons in it. III. The maximum coordination number in ionic solids is 8 .
questions.html:
Which of the following statements are true?
I. Ferrimagnetic material, Fe3O4 becomes paramagnetic at 850 K.
II. Paramagnetic behaviour of a material is due to the presence of the unpaired electrons in it.
III. The maximum coordination number in ionic solids is 8.
questions.questionHtml:
Which of the following statements are true?
I. Ferrimagnetic material, Fe3O4 becomes paramagnetic at 850 K.
II. Paramagnetic behaviour of a material is due to the presence of the unpaired electrons in it.
III. The maximum coordination number in ionic solids is 8.
questions.solutionText: Solution Statement I: F e 3 O 4 is ferrimagnetic at room temperature and transitions to paramagnetic above its Curie temperature, which is 850 K. → True Statement II: Paramagnetic behavior arises due to the presence of unpaired electrons, which create permanent magnetic dipole moments that align with an external field. → True Statement III: In ionic solids, the maximum coordination number is 8, as seen in C s C l (body-centered cubic type) where C s + is surrounded by 8 C l − ions. → True All three statements are correct. Answer: (4)
questions.solution: Solution Statement I: F e 3 O 4 is ferrimagnetic at room temperature and transitions to paramagnetic above its Curie temperature, which is 850 K. → True Statement II: Paramagnetic behavior arises due to the presence of unpaired electrons, which create permanent magnetic dipole moments that align with an external field. → True Statement III: In ionic solids, the maximum coordination number is 8, as seen in C s C l (body-centered cubic type) where C s + is surrounded by 8 C l − ions. → True All three statements are correct. Answer: (4)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Statement I: Fe3O4 is ferrimagnetic at room temperature and transitions to paramagnetic above its Curie temperature, which is 850 K. → True

Statement II: Paramagnetic behavior arises due to the presence of unpaired electrons, which create permanent magnetic dipole moments that align with an external field. → True

Statement III: In ionic solids, the maximum coordination number is 8, as seen in CsCl (body-centered cubic type) where Cs+ is surrounded by 8 Cl− ions. → True

All three statements are correct.

Answer: (4)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: PCl 5 exists but NCl 5 does not because
questions.questionText: PCl 5 exists but NCl 5 does not because
questions.question: PCl 5 exists but NCl 5 does not because
questions.prompt: PCl 5 exists but NCl 5 does not because
questions.html: PCl5 exists but NCl5 does not because
questions.questionHtml: PCl5 exists but NCl5 does not because
questions.solutionText: Solution P C l 5 exists because phosphorus belongs to the 3rd period and has vacant 3 d orbitals available for bonding, allowing it to expand its octet and form 5 bonds. Nitrogen belongs to the 2nd period and has no d orbitals in its valence shell. It cannot expand its octet beyond 4 bonds, so N C l 5 cannot be formed. Answer: (1)
questions.solution: Solution P C l 5 exists because phosphorus belongs to the 3rd period and has vacant 3 d orbitals available for bonding, allowing it to expand its octet and form 5 bonds. Nitrogen belongs to the 2nd period and has no d orbitals in its valence shell. It cannot expand its octet beyond 4 bonds, so N C l 5 cannot be formed. Answer: (1)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
PCl5 exists because phosphorus belongs to the 3rd period and has vacant 3d orbitals available for bonding, allowing it to expand its octet and form 5 bonds.

Nitrogen belongs to the 2nd period and has no d orbitals in its valence shell. It cannot expand its octet beyond 4 bonds, so NCl5 cannot be formed.

Answer: (1)

questions.prompt: Which of the following ligands forms a chelate
questions.html: Which of the following ligands forms a chelate
questions.questionHtml: Which of the following ligands forms a chelate
questions.solutionText: Solution In chelation, ring formation occurs because two atoms from the same ligand coordinate with the metal atom. This cannot happen if the ligand is monodentate. Oxalate is a bidentate ligand hence it forms a chelate. It can coordinate with both of its negatively charged O atoms. Acetate, cyanide and ammonia are monodentate ligands. They do not form chelates.

questions.solutionHtml:

In chelation, ring formation occurs because two atoms from the same ligand coordinate with the metal atom. This cannot happen if the ligand is monodentate.
Oxalate is a bidentate ligand hence it forms a chelate. It can coordinate with both of its negatively charged O atoms.
Acetate, cyanide and ammonia are monodentate ligands. They do not form chelates.

questions.solutionText: Solution Dissolution of Na 2 SO 4 in water is an exothermic process. When a substance dissolves in water by an exothermic process, its solubility decreases with an increase in temperature. Hence, solubility of Na 2 SO 4 in water decreases with increase in temperature.

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Dissolution of Na2SO4 in water is an exothermic process. When a substance dissolves in water by an exothermic process, its solubility decreases with an increase in temperature. Hence, solubility of Na2SO4 in water decreases with increase in temperature.

questions.questionHtml:
Identify the segment in the sentence that contains a grammatical error:
"Neither the total number of inhabitants in the coastal regions nor the density of the urban population are being considered by the committee in their final report."
questions.solutionText: Solution With 'neither...nor', the verb agrees with the subject closest to it (the noun nearest to the verb). Here, the subject closest to the verb is 'the density of the urban population', which is singular. So the verb must be 'is being considered', not 'are being considered'. The error lies in segment (3). Answer: (3)

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: Paragraph : Resin is a plant secretion that hardens when exposed to air; fossilized resin is called amber. Although Pliny in the first century recognized that amber was produced from "marrow discharged by trees," amber has been widely misunderstood to be a semiprecious gem and has even been described in mineralogy textbooks. Confusion also persists surrounding the term "resin," which was defined before rigorous chemical analyses were available. Resin is often confused with gum, a substance produced in plants in response to bacterial infections, and with sap, an aqueous solution transported through certain plant tissues. Resin differs from both gum and sap in that scientists have not determined a physiological function for resin. In the 1950s, entomologists posited that resin may function to repel or attract insects. Fraenkel conjectured that plants initially produced resin in nonspecific chemical responses to insect attack and that, over time, plants evolved that produced resin with specific repellent effects. But some insect species, he noted, might overcome the repellent effects, actually becoming attracted to the resin. This might induce the insects to feed on those plants or aid them in securing a breeding site. Later researchers suggested that resin mediates the complex interdependence, or "coevolution," of plants and insects over time. Such ideas led to the development of the specialized discipline of chemical ecology, which is concerned with the role of plant chemicals in interactions with other organisms and with the evolution and ecology of plant antiherbivore chemistry (plants' chemical defenses against attack by herbivores such as insects). Question The author of the passage refers to Pliny most probably in order to

questions.html:
Paragraph:
Resin is a plant secretion that hardens when exposed to air; fossilized resin is called amber. Although Pliny in the first century recognized that amber was produced from "marrow discharged by trees," amber has been widely misunderstood to be a semiprecious gem and has even been described in mineralogy textbooks. Confusion also persists surrounding the term "resin," which was defined before rigorous chemical analyses were available. Resin is often confused with gum, a substance produced in plants in response to bacterial infections, and with sap, an aqueous solution transported through certain plant tissues. Resin differs from both gum and sap in that scientists have not determined a physiological function for resin.
In the 1950s, entomologists posited that resin may function to repel or attract insects. Fraenkel conjectured that plants initially produced resin in nonspecific chemical responses to insect attack and that, over time, plants evolved that produced resin with specific repellent effects. But some insect species, he noted, might overcome the repellent effects, actually becoming attracted to the resin. This might induce the insects to feed on those plants or aid them in securing a breeding site. Later researchers suggested that resin mediates the complex interdependence, or "coevolution," of plants and insects over time. Such ideas led to the development of the specialized discipline of chemical ecology, which is concerned with the role of plant chemicals in interactions with other organisms and with the evolution and ecology of plant antiherbivore chemistry (plants' chemical defenses against attack by herbivores such as insects).

Question
The author of the passage refers to Pliny most probably in order to
questions.solutionText: Solution The passage states generally that amber bas been widely misunderstood but cites Pliny as noting correctly, in the first century, that amber resulted from a substance discharged by trees. A Pliny's observation was, according to the author, accurate and not a misunderstanding. B The author equates confusion about amber with confusion about resin; the reference to Pliny does not indicate which of the two, amber or resin, has been more widely misunderstood. C The author indicates that others, not Pliny, mischaracterized amber as a semiprecious gem-and when that mischaracterization first occurred is not identified. D Correct. Pliny's recognition that amber came from a substance discharged by trees stands, in the author's account, as an exception to the widespread incorrect identifications of the substance.
questions.solution: Solution The passage states generally that amber bas been widely misunderstood but cites Pliny as noting correctly, in the first century, that amber resulted from a substance discharged by trees. A Pliny's observation was, according to the author, accurate and not a misunderstanding. B The author equates confusion about amber with confusion about resin; the reference to Pliny does not indicate which of the two, amber or resin, has been more widely misunderstood. C The author indicates that others, not Pliny, mischaracterized amber as a semiprecious gem-and when that mischaracterization first occurred is not identified. D Correct. Pliny's recognition that amber came from a substance discharged by trees stands, in the author's account, as an exception to the widespread incorrect identifications of the substance.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The passage states generally that amber bas been widely misunderstood but cites Pliny as noting correctly, in the first century, that amber resulted from a substance discharged by trees.
A Pliny's observation was, according to the author, accurate and not a misunderstanding.
B The author equates confusion about amber with confusion about resin; the reference to Pliny does not indicate which of the two, amber or resin, has been more widely misunderstood.
C The author indicates that others, not Pliny, mischaracterized amber as a semiprecious gem-and when that mischaracterization first occurred is not identified.
D Correct. Pliny's recognition that amber came from a substance discharged by trees stands, in the author's account, as an exception to the widespread incorrect identifications of the substance.

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: Read the following passage carefully and answer the question given below it. Raghu has been orphaned when he was quite young so he had learned to fend for himself. One day he was very hungry. Wondering what to do, he happened to pass a sweet shop and heard the shopkeeper tell his young assistant, "Look after the shop while I take a nap. Call me only if something important comes up." Raghu smelt an opportunity. He waited for a short time and then entered. "I am the owner's best friend's son. Give me the best sweets in the shop." The boy was suspicious and asked him his name. "My name is Fly," said Raghu helping himself to some jalebis. As he sat eating, the boy ran to the owner, "Fly is eating the sweets. What should I do?" The sweet shop owner was in a deep sleep. He mumbled, "How much can a fly eat? Let him be." Then he turned over and started snoring loudly. The poor boy could do nothing but watch him demolish a plate of jalebis. Finally Raghu took two boxes of the best sweets in the shop and left without paying. These boxes he presented to Mahadev saying, "Here is a present for our city's greatest man. Please accept this. I am a poor man and have nothing else to give you." Seeing the box full of expensive sweets Mahadev, who rarely parted with money, was delighted. "Sir", continued Raghu, "I have only one request. May I have the empty boxes back please?" Mahadev was only interested in the sweets so he said graciously, "Of course. Ask my wife to give you two vessels for the sweets." Raghu went to Mahadev's wife and said, "Your husband has asked you to give me two gold coins". The woman who was cooking lunch was astonished and said, "Are you out of your mind?" My husband would never say such a thing!" So Raghu called out to Mahadev, "Your wife is refusing to give me what I asked for." Mahadev was negotiating a handsome deal in his shop. Irritated by the disturbance he called out, "Give him what he wants." So Raghu left with the gold coins in his pocket. Days went by and Raghu made his living through his smooth talk and tall promise. The king heard about this. Raghu was brought to him under arrest. The king heard to his story in silence and then said, "Since you are so clever study with the best pundits in the land. Use your wits for the betterment of the kingdom." Raghu was silent. No one had praised him or offered the King's terms and one day came to be known as one of the wisest of his ministers. Why did the boy allow Raghu to leave without paying for the sweets? (I) He did not want to offend a good customer by insisting on payment (II) He did not want to start an argument and wake up the owner. (III) He saw how hungry Raghu was and felt sorry for him

Call me only if something important comes up." Raghu smelt an opportunity. He waited for a short time and then entered. "I am the owner's best friend's son. Give me the best sweets in the shop." The boy was suspicious and asked him his name. "My name is Fly," said Raghu helping himself to some jalebis. As he sat eating, the boy ran to the owner, "Fly is eating the sweets. What should I do?" The sweet shop owner was in a deep sleep. He mumbled, "How much can a fly eat? Let him be." Then he turned over and started snoring loudly. The poor boy could do nothing but watch him demolish a plate of jalebis. Finally Raghu took two boxes of the best sweets in the shop and left without paying. These boxes he presented to Mahadev saying, "Here is a present for our city's greatest man. Please accept this. I am a poor man and have nothing else to give you." Seeing the box full of expensive sweets Mahadev, who rarely parted with money, was delighted. "Sir", continued Raghu, "I have only one request. May I have the empty boxes back please?" Mahadev was only interested in the sweets so he said graciously, "Of course. Ask my wife to give you two vessels for the sweets." Raghu went to Mahadev's wife and said, "Your husband has asked you to give me two gold coins". The woman who was cooking lunch was astonished and said, "Are you out of your mind?" My husband would never say such a thing!" So Raghu called out to Mahadev, "Your wife is refusing to give me what I asked for." Mahadev was negotiating a handsome deal in his shop. Irritated by the disturbance he called out, "Give him what he wants." So Raghu left with the gold coins in his pocket. Days went by and Raghu made his living through his smooth talk and tall promise. The king heard about this. Raghu was brought to him under arrest. The king heard to his story in silence and then said, "Since you are so clever study with the best pundits in the land. Use your wits for the betterment of the kingdom." Raghu was silent. No one had praised him or offered the King's terms and one day came to be known as one of the wisest of his ministers. Why did the boy allow Raghu to leave without paying for the sweets? (I) He did not want to offend a good customer by insisting on payment (II) He did not want to start an argument and wake up the owner. (III) He saw how hungry Raghu was and felt sorry for him
questions.html:
Read the following passage carefully and answer the question given below it.

Raghu has been orphaned when he was quite young so he had learned to fend for himself. One day he was very hungry. Wondering what to do, he happened to pass a sweet shop and heard the shopkeeper tell his young assistant, "Look after the shop while I take a nap. Call me only if something important comes up." Raghu smelt an opportunity. He waited for a short time and then entered. "I am the owner's best friend's son. Give me the best sweets in the shop." The boy was suspicious and asked him his name. "My name is Fly," said Raghu helping himself to some jalebis. As he sat eating, the boy ran to the owner, "Fly is eating the sweets. What should I do?" The sweet shop owner was in a deep sleep. He mumbled, "How much can a fly eat? Let him be." Then he turned over and started snoring loudly. The poor boy could do nothing but watch him demolish a plate of jalebis. Finally Raghu took two boxes of the best sweets in the shop and left without paying.

These boxes he presented to Mahadev saying, "Here is a present for our city's greatest man. Please accept this. I am a poor man and have nothing else to give you." Seeing the box full of expensive sweets Mahadev, who rarely parted with money, was delighted. "Sir", continued Raghu, "I have only one request. May I have the empty boxes back please?" Mahadev was only interested in the sweets so he said graciously, "Of course. Ask my wife to give you two vessels for the sweets." Raghu went to Mahadev's wife and said, "Your husband has asked you to give me two gold coins". The woman who was cooking lunch was astonished and said, "Are you out of your mind?" My husband would never say such a thing!" So Raghu called out to Mahadev, "Your wife is refusing to give me what I asked for." Mahadev was negotiating a handsome deal in his shop. Irritated by the disturbance he called out, "Give him what he wants." So Raghu left with the gold coins in his pocket.

Days went by and Raghu made his living through his smooth talk and tall promise. The king heard about this. Raghu was brought to him under arrest. The king heard to his story in silence and then said, "Since you are so clever study with the best pundits in the land. Use your wits for the betterment of the kingdom." Raghu was silent. No one had praised him or offered the King's terms and one day came to be known as one of the wisest of his ministers.

Why did the boy allow Raghu to leave without paying for the sweets?
(I) He did not want to offend a good customer by insisting on payment
(II) He did not want to start an argument and wake up the owner.
(III) He saw how hungry Raghu was and felt sorry for him

My husband would never say such a thing!" So Raghu called out to Mahadev, "Your wife is refusing to give me what I asked for." Mahadev was negotiating a handsome deal in his shop. Irritated by the disturbance he called out, "Give him what he wants." So Raghu left with the gold coins in his pocket.

Days went by and Raghu made his living through his smooth talk and tall promise. The king heard about this. Raghu was brought to him under arrest. No one had praised him or offered the King's terms and one day came to be known as one of the wisest of his ministers.

Why did the boy allow Raghu to leave without paying for the sweets?
(I) He did not want to offend a good customer by insisting on payment
(II) He did not want to start an argument and wake up the owner.
(III) He saw how hungry Raghu was and felt sorry for him
questions.solutionText: Solution The boy ran to the owner to report that Fly was eating the sweets. The owner was in a deep sleep and mumbled, 'How much can a fly eat? Let him be,' and went back to snoring. The boy could do nothing but watch Raghu eat the jalebis. He did not act because the owner, when disturbed, dismissed the matter and went back to sleep. Waking him again would mean starting an argument and disturbing his nap further. Answer: (II) He did not want to start an argument and wake up the owner.
questions.solution: Solution The boy ran to the owner to report that Fly was eating the sweets. The owner was in a deep sleep and mumbled, 'How much can a fly eat? Let him be,' and went back to snoring. The boy could do nothing but watch Raghu eat the jalebis. He did not act because the owner, when disturbed, dismissed the matter and went back to sleep. Waking him again would mean starting an argument and disturbing his nap further. Answer: (II) He did not want to start an argument and wake up the owner.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The boy ran to the owner to report that Fly was eating the sweets. The owner was in a deep sleep and mumbled, 'How much can a fly eat? Let him be,' and went back to snoring.

The boy could do nothing but watch Raghu eat the jalebis. He did not act because the owner, when disturbed, dismissed the matter and went back to sleep. Waking him again would mean starting an argument and disturbing his nap further.

Answer: (II) He did not want to start an argument and wake up the owner.

questions.questionHtml:
Choose the word that is most nearly the OPPOSITE in meaning to the given word:

DILIGENT
questions.solutionText: Solution DILIGENT means hardworking and careful. A) Industrious → synonym of diligent C) Meticulous → synonym (careful, thorough) D) Cautious → related but not opposite B) Lethargic → means sluggish, lacking energy → opposite of diligent Answer: B) Lethargic
questions.solution: Solution DILIGENT means hardworking and careful. A) Industrious → synonym of diligent C) Meticulous → synonym (careful, thorough) D) Cautious → related but not opposite B) Lethargic → means sluggish, lacking energy → opposite of diligent Answer: B) Lethargic
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
DILIGENT means hardworking and careful.

A) Industrious → synonym of diligent
C) Meticulous → synonym (careful, thorough)
D) Cautious → related but not opposite

B) Lethargic → means sluggish, lacking energy → opposite of diligent

Answer: B) Lethargic

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: Paragraph Economic growth involves both benefits and costs. The desirability of increasing production has frequently been challenged in recent years and some have even mentioned that economic growth is merely a quantitative enlargement that has no human meaning or value. However, economic growth is an increase in the capacity to produce goods and service that people want. Since the product of economic growth can be measured by its value to someone, it is important to ask whose standard of valuation counts. In the U.S. the value of a product is what purchasers pay for it. That is determined by the purchaser's preferences combined with condition of supply which in turn reflects various other factors, such as natural and technological circumstances of those who supply capital and labour. The value by which we measure a product, synthesize all these factors. Gross National Product (GNP) is the market value of the nation's total output of goods and service. GNP is not a perfect measure of all the activities involved in economic output. It does not account for the deterioration or improvement in the environment even when they are incidentally results of the production process. On the other hand, it does not count as "Product", many benefits provided as side effect of the economic process. It does not include productive but unpaid work (such as that done by a home maker) and it does not reckon with such other factor as the burdensomeness of work, the length of the work, week and so forth. Nonetheless the GNP concept makes an important contribution to our understanding of how the economy is working while it is not a complete measure of economic productivity and even less so of "welfare". The level and rate of the increase of GNP are clearly and positively associated with what most people throughout the world see as an improvement in the quality of life. In the long run, the same factor results in a growing GNP and in other social benefits: size and competence of population, state of knowledge, amount of capital and the effectiveness with those are combined and Utilized. Question The main purpose of the passage is to :

Paragraph
Economic growth involves both benefits and costs. The desirability of increasing production has frequently been challenged in recent years and some have even mentioned that economic growth is merely a quantitative enlargement that has no human meaning or value.

However, economic growth is an increase in the capacity to produce goods and service that people want. Since the product of economic growth can be measured by its value to someone, it is important to ask whose standard of valuation counts.

In the U.S. the value of a product is what purchasers pay for it. questions.solutionText: Solution The passage begins by acknowledging criticism of economic growth, then counters it by explaining that economic growth increases the capacity to produce goods people want, introduces GNP as a measure of this output, and concludes that rising GNP is positively associated with improved quality of life. The overall thrust is to defend and argue for the value of increased economic output, not merely to define GNP or discuss disadvantages of material measurement. Answer: (1)

The overall thrust is to defend and argue for the value of increased economic output, not merely to define GNP or discuss disadvantages of material measurement. Answer: (1)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The passage begins by acknowledging criticism of economic growth, then counters it by explaining that economic growth increases the capacity to produce goods people want, introduces GNP as a measure of this output, and concludes that rising GNP is positively associated with improved quality of life.

The overall thrust is to defend and argue for the value of increased economic output, not merely to define GNP or discuss disadvantages of material measurement.

Answer: (1)

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.html: 4, 60, 325, 1316, ?, 7918, 7919
questions.questionHtml: 4, 60, 325, 1316, ?, 7918, 7919
questions.solutionText: Solution 4, 60, 325, 1316, ?, 7918, 7919 4 = 5 1 − 1 60 = 5 2 × 12 5 ... let me try another pattern. 4 × 15 = 60 60 × 5 + 25 = 325 325 × 4 + 16 = 1316 1316 × 3 + 9 = 3957 3957 × 2 + 4 = 7918 7918 × 1 + 1 = 7919 The multiplier decreases by 1 and the added term is a perfect square decreasing: + 25 , + 16 , + 9 , + 4 , + 1 So the missing term = 1316 × 3 + 9 = 3948 + 9 = 3957 Answer: (2)
questions.solution: Solution 4, 60, 325, 1316, ?, 7918, 7919 4 = 5 1 − 1 60 = 5 2 × 12 5 ... let me try another pattern. 4 × 15 = 60 60 × 5 + 25 = 325 325 × 4 + 16 = 1316 1316 × 3 + 9 = 3957 3957 × 2 + 4 = 7918 7918 × 1 + 1 = 7919 The multiplier decreases by 1 and the added term is a perfect square decreasing: + 25 , + 16 , + 9 , + 4 , + 1 So the missing term = 1316 × 3 + 9 = 3948 + 9 = 3957 Answer: (2)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
4, 60, 325, 1316, ?, 7918, 7919

4=51−1
60=52×125... let me try another pattern.

4×15=60
60×5+25=325
325×4+16=1316
1316×3+9=3957
3957×2+4=7918
7918×1+1=7919

The multiplier decreases by 1 and the added term is a perfect square decreasing: +25,+16,+9,+4,+1

So the missing term =1316×3+9=3948+9=3957

Answer: (2)

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: What is the next number in the following series? 12 , 14 , 21 , 37 , 66 , 112 , ?
questions.questionText: What is the next number in the following series? 12 , 14 , 21 , 37 , 66 , 112 , ?
questions.question: What is the next number in the following series? 12 , 14 , 21 , 37 , 66 , 112 , ?
questions.prompt: What is the next number in the following series? 12 , 14 , 21 , 37 , 66 , 112 , ?
questions.html:
What is the next number in the following series?
12,14,21,37,66,112,?
questions.questionHtml:
What is the next number in the following series?
12,14,21,37,66,112,?
questions.solutionText: Solution Series: 12, 14, 21, 37, 66, 112, ? Differences: 2, 7, 16, 29, 46 Second differences: 5, 9, 13, 17 → increasing by 4 Next second difference = 21 Next first difference = 46 + 21 = 67 Next term = 112 + 67 = 179 Answer: (2) 179
questions.solution: Solution Series: 12, 14, 21, 37, 66, 112, ? Differences: 2, 7, 16, 29, 46 Second differences: 5, 9, 13, 17 → increasing by 4 Next second difference = 21 Next first difference = 46 + 21 = 67 Next term = 112 + 67 = 179 Answer: (2) 179
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Series: 12, 14, 21, 37, 66, 112, ?

Differences: 2, 7, 16, 29, 46

Second differences: 5, 9, 13, 17 → increasing by 4

Next second difference =21
Next first difference =46+21=67
Next term =112+67=179

Answer: (2) 179

questions.solutionText: Solution In the figures given in the question, the digits of the number in the centre of the circle have been formed by multiplying the numbers in opposite sides. The first digit is formed by the multiplication of middle digits and the second digit by the multiplication of upper right and lower left digits and so on. Thus, in the first figure, the digits of the number 584 are formed as, 5 × 1 = 5 , 4 × 2 = 8 and 2 × 2 = 4 In the second figure, the digits of the number 694 are formed as, 3 × 2 = 6 , 3 × 3 = 9 and 2 × 2 = 4 In the same way, the three digits of the number in the third figure will be, 6 × 1 = 6 , 7 × 1 = 7 and 4 × 2 = 8 Thus, the missing number in the third figure will be 678 .

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
In the figures given in the question, the digits of the number in the centre of the circle have been formed by multiplying the numbers in opposite sides. The first digit is formed by the multiplication of middle digits and the second digit by the multiplication of upper right and lower left digits and so on.
Thus, in the first figure, the digits of the number 584 are formed as,
5×1=5,
4×2=8 and
2×2=4
In the second figure, the digits of the number 694 are formed as,
3×2=6,
3×3=9 and
2×2=4
In the same way, the three digits of the number in the third figure will be,
6×1=6,
7×1=7 and
4×2=8
Thus, the missing number in the third figure will be 678.

questions.text: Read the given statement and the following conclusions carefully. Decide which of the conclusions logically follows from the statement. Statement: The company's overall profit has dropped by 15% this quarter, despite a 10% increase in the total sales volume of its products. Conclusions: I. The cost of production or operational expenses must have increased during this quarter. II. The company should immediately reduce its workforce to recover the lost profit.

Statement: The company's overall profit has dropped by 15% this quarter, despite a 10% increase in the total sales volume of its products.

questions.questionHtml:
Read the given statement and the following conclusions carefully. Decide which of the conclusions logically follows from the statement.

Statement: The company's overall profit has dropped by 15% this quarter, despite a 10% increase in the total sales volume of its products.

Conclusions:
I. The cost of production or operational expenses must have increased during this quarter.
II. The company should immediately reduce its workforce to recover the lost profit.
questions.solutionText: Solution Sales volume increased by 10% but profit dropped by 15%. Conclusion I: Costs MUST have increased — not necessarily true. Profit could have fallen due to a reduction in selling price, not just rising costs. So this does not definitively follow. Conclusion II: Reducing workforce is an assumption with no basis in the statement. Does not follow. Answer: D) Neither conclusion I nor II follows
questions.solution: Solution Sales volume increased by 10% but profit dropped by 15%. Conclusion I: Costs MUST have increased — not necessarily true. Profit could have fallen due to a reduction in selling price, not just rising costs. So this does not definitively follow. Conclusion II: Reducing workforce is an assumption with no basis in the statement. Does not follow. Answer: D) Neither conclusion I nor II follows
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Sales volume increased by 10% but profit dropped by 15%.

Conclusion I: Costs MUST have increased — not necessarily true. Profit could have fallen due to a reduction in selling price, not just rising costs. So this does not definitively follow.

Conclusion II: Reducing workforce is an assumption with no basis in the statement. Does not follow.

Answer: D) Neither conclusion I nor II follows

questions.solutionText: Solution We have to use Venn diagram for solve this statement. So statement are; Some tables are tree, and some trees are flowers, all flowers are jungles. As we see conclusion then we got; Some jungles are tables is fasle conclusion. some trees are jungles is true. some flowers are tables is false and all jungles are flowers is false. So we can sat that only second follows statement. Thus answer is "only 2 follows".

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

We have to use Venn diagram for solve this statement.

So statement are;

Some tables are tree, and some trees are flowers, all flowers are jungles.

As we see conclusion then we got;

Some jungles are tables is fasle conclusion.

some trees are jungles is true.

some flowers are tables is false and all jungles are flowers is false.

So we can sat that only second follows statement.

Thus answer is "only 2 follows".

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: A , B , C , D and E are sitting on a bench. A is sitting next to B , C is sitting next to D , D is not sitting with E who is on the left end of the bench. C is on the second position from the right. A is to the right of B and E . A and C are sitting together. In which position A is sitting?
questions.questionText: A , B , C , D and E are sitting on a bench. A is sitting next to B , C is sitting next to D , D is not sitting with E who is on the left end of the bench.
